make_pbt:
All 3 tests pass.

## Summary

Added two property-based tests to `src/lib.rs`, both drawing from a shared `pair_strategy` that generates pairs of `Vec<i64>` with values bounded to `i32::MIN..=i32::MAX` (so the absolute difference always fits in `i64` — the contract is only meaningful in that range).

### Contract clauses captured

| Test | Clause | Type |
|------|--------|------|
| `length_is_min_of_inputs` | `result.len() == min(scores.len(), guesses.len())` | Postcondition (length) |
| `element_is_absolute_difference` | `result[i] == (scores[i] - guesses[i]).abs()` for all valid `i` | Postcondition (value at each index) |

### Preconditions / failure modes
The function has no explicit preconditions (any slices are accepted, including empty ones — the length test exercises both). The only implicit precondition is "the absolute difference fits in `i64`"; outside this range the `s[i] - g[i]` subtraction would overflow. We model this by bounding the input range rather than writing a separate "panics on overflow" test, since the failure is a property of `i64` arithmetic, not of `compare` itself.

### Properties intentionally omitted (derived facts)

- **Non-negativity of every output element** — follows directly from "result equals absolute value", so testing it adds a theorem downstream without adding information.
- **Symmetry: `compare(s, g) == compare(g, s)`** — follows from `|s - g| == |g - s|`, a property of absolute value, not of `compare`.
- **Zero diagonal: `compare(s, s)` is all zeros** — a special case of the element-wise postcondition.
- **Empty input gives empty output** — subsumed by the length test (when either input has length 0).
- **Length is exactly one of `scores.len()` or `guesses.len()`** — implied by the `min` length test plus basic arithmetic.

Each kept test would catch a distinct class of bug (wrong length truncation vs. wrong per-element formula); each dropped test would still pass on any implementation satisfying the two kept ones.
rust_hax_lean:
All three stages pass cleanly:

- `cargo test` — 3/3 pass (known + 2 proptests)
- `cargo hax into lean` — extraction succeeds
- `lake build` — `Built clever_150_compare`, no warnings, no `sorry`

## Final report

### Error driving the change

`lake build` failure on the original source:
```
error: clever_150_compare.lean:38:7: Unknown identifier `alloc.vec.Impl_1.push`
```

`Vec::push` (`alloc.vec.Impl_1.push`) is unmodeled in the Hax Lean prelude — only `Vec::new`, `Vec::len`, and `Vec::extend_from_slice` are available.

### Change applied

Inside `build_at`, replaced `acc.push(d)` with the typed-let + `extend_from_slice` pattern from `rewrite_patterns/vec_push_to_extend_from_slice_typed_chunk.rs`:

```rust
// before
acc.push(d);

// after
let chunk: [i64; 1] = [d];
acc.extend_from_slice(&chunk);
```

The explicit `[i64; 1]` type ascription is load-bearing — it surfaces in the extracted Lean as `RustArray i64 1`, letting `rust_primitives.unsize chunk` resolve the size metavariable (per the archive pattern's second gotcha).

### Hax-degenerate pattern checks (all clear)

- **Recursion shape**: `build_at` is already tail-recursive (no `while` loop, no `loop_invariant!` / `loop_decreases!`); extracts as `partial_fixpoint`. The proof stage gets the recursion-preferred shape suited to `Nat.strongRecOn` on `s.len() - i`.
- **Short-circuit `&&` / `||` over partial ops**: the outer guard `i >= s.len() || i >= g.len()` is `||` over two total comparisons — no partial op on either side. The `if s[i] >= g[i] { s[i] - g[i] } else { g[i] - s[i] }` keeps both partial subtractions strictly inside an `if`/`else`, which Hax preserves.
- **Library calls inlined**: `Vec::push` → `extend_from_slice`-of-typed-array (the only inline replacement).
- **Patterns considered but not changed**: nothing else; `Vec::new`, slice indexing (`s[i]` / `g[i]`), `slice::len`, and tail recursion all extract cleanly.
- **Suspected semantic changes**: none. `extend_from_slice(&[d])` appends a single element to `acc` exactly as `push(d)` did; tests (proptests for length + element-wise absolute difference) continue to pass.
- **Unresolved Hax-degenerate patterns**: none — the extracted Lean has no `sorry`, no `RustM.fail` divergence under any test-reachable input, and no unmodeled identifiers.

equivalence_check:
## Summary

The only change between the original and the Hax-rewritten version is in `build_at`: the call `acc.push(d)` was replaced with a typed 1-element chunk plus `extend_from_slice`:

```rust
let chunk: [i64; 1] = [d];
acc.extend_from_slice(&chunk);
```

This is annotated as a Hax-prelude workaround (no model for `Vec::push`).

## Reasoning

`Vec::push(d)` appends a single element `d` to the end of `acc`. `acc.extend_from_slice(&[d])` iterates the slice (one element) and appends each by cloning — for `i64` (which is `Copy`), cloning is identity. The resulting `Vec` has the same length and element values in the same order as if `push(d)` had been called.

- No reordering: `extend_from_slice` appends in slice order; with a 1-element slice there's only one element to append.
- No type or value change: `d: i64` is placed in `[i64; 1]` and then appended as `i64`.
- No panic/overflow difference: both `push` and `extend_from_slice` may reallocate identically; neither introduces new failure modes for this input domain.
- Surrounding control flow (recursion, base case, `d` computation via the inline `if s[i] >= g[i]` for absolute difference) is byte-identical.

All other code (the `compare` wrapper and tests) is unchanged.

For every input, `compare(scores, guesses)` produces the same `Vec<i64>` as before.

VERDICT: PASS
select_examples:
Looking at the target `clever_150_compare`:

**Lean shape:** `partial_fixpoint` tail-recursive `build_at s g i acc` over **two i64 slices** with **dual bounds check** `i >= s.len() OR i >= g.len()`, per-step computing `|s[i] - g[i]|` via **explicit if/else branching** (`if s[i] >= g[i] then s[i] - g[i] else g[i] - s[i]`), pushed to a `Vec<i64>` via `extend_from_slice` with a typed `[i64; 1]` chunk, then recursing on `(i+1, acc)`.

**Contract obstacles to discharge:**
- `partial_fixpoint` recursion with strong induction on `min(s.size, g.size) - i.toNat`
- Per-element signed i64 subtraction overflow (the bounded proptest range pre-empts this — needs hfit-style precondition)
- Vec construction via `[i64;1]` chunk + elementwise output invariant `acc.val[j].toInt = |s[j].toInt - g[j].toInt|`
- Length contract `result.len() = min(s.len(), g.len())`
- usize +? 1 no-overflow

## Selected examples

```
proof_patterns/clever_009_rolling_max_modified
proof_patterns/abs_diff_i64_modified
proof_patterns/clever_025_remove_duplicates_modified
proof_patterns/clever_057_common_modified
proof_patterns/clever_121_add_elements_modified
```

**Rationales:**

- **clever_009_rolling_max_modified** — Tightest structural match: tail-recursive i64-slice scan building `Vec<i64>` via `extend_from_slice` with `[i64;1]` typed chunk; carries the canonical *elementwise output invariant* `acc.val[j].toInt = <oracle> j`. The target's invariant will be the same shape with the oracle = abs-diff at index j.
- **abs_diff_i64_modified** — Exactly the per-element `if a >= b then a - b else b - a` branch with paired success/failure theorems per arm; demonstrates the `BitVec.ssubOverflow` + `Int64.le_iff_toInt_le`/`lt_iff_toInt_lt` + `omega` discharge needed for the two i64 subtractions inside the abs-diff branch.
- **clever_025_remove_duplicates_modified** — Closest `partial_fixpoint` Vec-builder with `[i64;1]` extend_from_slice; shares the strong-induction-with-RustM-ok-result discharge pattern (`generalize hres :` then `cases` on the result to propagate `fail`/`div` by contradiction) that the target's `partial_fixpoint` recursion will need.
- **clever_057_common_modified** — One of the few entries taking **two i64 slices** as input simultaneously; shows the typing/proof bookkeeping for `RustSlice i64` × `RustSlice i64` and the `push_one` Vec-extension helper that's also useful for the target's per-iteration single-element push.
- **clever_121_add_elements_modified** — Provides the **dual-exit-condition-iteration** template (`i ≥ k OR i ≥ arr.size`) which mirrors the target's `i >= s.len() || i >= g.len()` guard exactly; oracle `cond_sum_int_eq_of_ge` collapses past either boundary in a single stability lemma — the target needs the analogous `min(len_s, len_g)` stability.

## Gaps

- **Synchronous pairwise iteration over two slices at the same index** — no existing entry does this; clever_057 keeps the two slices in *asymmetric* roles (one iterated, one membership-queried), and clever_020/000 are *all-pairs over a single slice*. The target's "advance both indices in lockstep" pattern is novel for the library.
- **`min(len_s, len_g)` length characterization** as the dual-exit terminator — clever_121's dual-exit involves a user-supplied bound vs slice length; the symmetric two-slice-length case isn't covered.
- **Per-element `natAbs` postcondition without an aggregating oracle** — existing abs-handling entries (clever_004, clever_121) aggregate; the target wants pointwise equality `out[i] = (s[i] - g[i]).abs()`.

## Rejections

- **clever_004_mean_absolute_deviation_modified** — Abs computed via unary-neg + natAbs aggregate; target uses subtraction-branch instead. Aggregating not pointwise.
- **clever_020_find_closest_elements_modified** — All-pairs scan with multiplicative 2D measure; target is single-pointer pairwise sync.
- **clever_032 / clever_036 / clever_069 / clever_087 / clever_103 / clever_115 / clever_119** — Insertion-sort/rebuild pipelines, much heavier than needed.
- **intersperse_modified** — Vec build but with mixed 1/2-chunk branches and parity-based positional formula; structurally divergent.
- **rescale_to_unit_modified** — Two-pass extremum-then-shift; the second pass *is* per-element Vec construction but the spec shape (single-slice transform) differs.
- **sum_product_modified / sum_squares_132_modified / clever_113** — Scalar accumulator outputs, no Vec construction.
- **recursion_example** — Trivial partial_fixpoint; no Vec or abs structure.
make_lean_obligations:
Now I have a clear picture. Let me check the current state of the obligations file and the extracted module's exact signature:
Now I'll write the obligations file. The two property tests correspond to:
1. **Length postcondition** — `out.len() = min(scores.len(), guesses.len())`
2. **Element postcondition** — `out[i] = |scores[i] - guesses[i]|`

The function can fail on i64 subtraction overflow (proptest's bounded `i32::MIN..=i32::MAX` range pre-empts this), but conditioning theorems on `hres : compare s g = RustM.ok v` is the right framing — vacuously true on overflow inputs, substantive on success. This matches the rolling_max reference pattern exactly.
Only `sorry` warnings, no errors. Let me confirm with `lake build`:
Build succeeds with only the expected `sorry` warnings.
## Summary

**File edited:** `proofs/lean/extraction/Clever_150_compareObligations.lean`

**Theorems added (both `sorry`-placeholder, well-typed under `lake build`):**

1. `compare_length` — Length postcondition: `compare s g = ok v → v.val.size = min s.val.size g.val.size`. Captures proptest `length_is_min_of_inputs`.
2. `compare_element_is_abs_difference` — Element postcondition: `compare s g = ok v → ∀ i < v.val.size, v[i].toInt = (s[i].toInt - g[i].toInt).natAbs`. Captures proptest `element_is_absolute_difference`.

**No derived facts skipped.** Both proptests are direct contract clauses.

**Framing rationale.** I followed the reference style of `clever_009_rolling_max` exactly: condition each postcondition on `hres : compare s g = RustM.ok v` rather than making the function totality a separate antecedent. The Rust function can fail on i64 subtraction overflow (e.g., `s[i] = i64::MAX, g[i] = i64::MIN`), and the proptest's bounded `i32::MIN..=i32::MAX` range pre-empts this — so the universal "for all i64 slices" totality claim is *false* in the Lean model. By conditioning on `hres`, the theorems are:
- **vacuously true** on overflow inputs (where the LHS hypothesis fails), and
- **substantively true** on success inputs (where the subtractions fit and the contract holds).

This preserves the contract surface without introducing a brittle `hfit`-style precondition.

**Notes / uncertainty.**
- The element theorem uses `.natAbs : Int → Nat` on the RHS with an implicit `ℕ → ℤ` coercion to match the `.toInt : Int` LHS — this typechecks under `lake build`.
- I did not add a separate `compare_total` totality theorem (with an `hfit`-style precondition like `∀ i < min, (s[i].toInt - g[i].toInt).natAbs < 2^63`). It would mirror the `rolling_max_total` pattern from the reference and is structurally feasible, but is not required by any proptest. If the proof stage finds the conditional postconditions easier to discharge by routing through such a totality lemma, it can introduce it locally.

## Summary

**File edited:** `proofs/lean/extraction/Clever_150_compareObligations.lean`

Both public obligations — `compare_length` and `compare_element_is_abs_difference` — are closed; `lake build` reports success with no `sorry` and no errors/warnings.

### Proof architecture (private helpers)

1. **Standard scaffolding** — `RustM_ok_bind`, `usize_one_toNat`, `usize_size_eq`, `one_lt_usize_size`, `usize_add_one_toNat`, `usize_add_one_no_bv`. Lifted verbatim from the `clever_009_rolling_max` reference.
2. **`push_one`** (+ `push_one_size`) — packages `acc ++ #[x]` for the `extend_from_slice` of a typed 1-element chunk.
3. **`build_at_oob`** — closes the dual exit condition `i ≥ s.len() || i ≥ g.len()`. Standard `decide` reductions through `core_models.slice.Impl.len`, `rust_primitives.cmp.ge`, and `rust_primitives.hax.logical_op.or`.
4. **Four in-range step lemmas**:
   - `build_at_step_left` — `s[i] ≥ g[i]` and no overflow → step with chunk `s[i] - g[i]`.
   - `build_at_step_right` — `¬(s[i] ≥ g[i])` and no overflow → step with chunk `g[i] - s[i]`.
   - `build_at_step_left_fail` — `s[i] ≥ g[i]` with overflow → `fail integerOverflow`.
   - `build_at_step_right_fail` — analogous for the right arm.
   Each reduces the body through the index/compare/sub/unsize/extend chain via a long `simp only`.
5. **`build_at_correct`** — strong induction on `min s.val.size g.val.size - i.toNat`, carrying:
   - `acc.val.size = i.toNat`,
   - elementwise invariant `(acc.val[j]).toInt = ((s.val[j].toInt - g.val[j].toInt)).natAbs`.
   The successor case dispatches on `s[i] ≥ g[i]` × `subOverflow`, using the fail variants to contradict `hres = ok v` and the success variants to apply the IH.
6. **`compare_aux`** — specialises `build_at_correct` at `i = 0`, `acc = Vec.new`; both top-level obligations destructure its conjunction.

### Notable sub-goals and how they were resolved

- **`congr 1` failed silently** on the dependent-getElem equation `s.val[acc.val.size]'hjs = s.val[i.toNat]'his` — replaced with `getElem_congr_idx h_acc_size_eq_i`, the standard Lean-core helper used by `clever_025`.
- **No Mathlib** — replaced `ring` and `by_contra` with `omega` and an explicit `intro` + `Int64.le_iff_toInt_le` bridge respectively. Replaced the Mathlib-only `set` tactic with explicit `(push_one acc ...)` references throughout.
- **`natAbs` on the right arm** — collapsed by `Int.natAbs_neg` followed by `Int.natAbs_of_nonneg` on `g[i].toInt - s[i].toInt`, which is nonneg because `¬(s[i] ≥ g[i])` lifts to `s[i].toInt < g[i].toInt` via `Int64.le_iff_toInt_le`.

### Obligations left as `sorry`

None. Both `compare_length` and `compare_element_is_abs_difference` are fully closed.
harvest_proof_patterns:
Looking at this target, I need to assess whether the proof contributes new obstacle coverage to the library.

**Structural analysis of the proof:**

1. The function iterates over TWO slices in PARALLEL at the same index, terminating when either runs out (`i ≥ s.len() || i ≥ g.len()`), pushing `|s[i] - g[i]|` per step
2. The induction measure is `min(s.val.size, g.val.size) - i.toNat` — neither slice's size alone
3. The OOB step lemma handles a DISJUNCTIVE condition `s.val.size ≤ i.toNat ∨ g.val.size ≤ i.toNat`
4. The body branches on `s[i] ≥ g[i]` to dispatch between two distinct subtractions (`s[i] - g[i]` vs `g[i] - s[i]`), each with its own overflow discharge — TWO arms × {success, fail} = 4 step lemmas
5. The element-output invariant ties to BOTH input slices: `acc.val[j].toInt = (s.val[j].toInt - g.val[j].toInt).natAbs`

**Comparison with closest library entries:**

- `clever_009_rolling_max_modified` / `clever_025_remove_duplicates_modified` / `intersperse_modified`: SINGLE-slice scan with `extend_from_slice [i64;1]` chunk — no parallel two-slice iteration
- `abs_diff_i64_modified`: scalar `a-b if a>b else b-a` with signed-subtraction overflow — but a single computation, no slice scan
- `clever_057_common_modified` / `clever_046_median_modified`: operate on TWO slices but sequentially (outer iterates l1, inner queries l2) — not parallel elementwise at same i
- `clever_072_smallest_change_modified`: two-slice symmetry bridge but as a postcondition on a single-slice scan, not parallel iteration
- `clever_004_mean_absolute_deviation_modified`: has `absolute-value-branching` + sign-conditioned dispatch but on a single slice with mean computed in pass 1

The **two-slice parallel elementwise scan with min-of-two-sizes termination measure and disjunctive OOB condition** appears structurally absent from the library. Combined with the per-step sign-conditioned subtraction (lifting `abs_diff_i64`'s scalar shape into a slice scan), this is a genuinely new obstacle composition.

Adding the entry.
Name needs to start with a letter. Following the library convention (`clever_NNN_<name>_modified`):
ADDED `clever_150_compare_modified` — features include `two-slice-parallel-elementwise-scan`, `min-of-two-sizes-induction-measure`, `disjunctive-oob-condition`, `two-arm-sign-conditioned-subtraction`, `paired-success-failure-per-arm-step-lemmas`, `natAbs-elementwise-output-invariant`, plus the standard `extend-from-slice-step` / `elementwise-output-invariant` / `signed-overflow` / `absolute-value-branching` machinery. Summary: tail-recursive parallel scan over two i64 slices producing `out[i] = |s[i] - g[i]|` of length `min(s.size, g.size)`, with disjunctive OOB termination and per-arm sign-conditioned subtraction overflow dispatch.

Notable observations: this is the library's first entry where **two slices are iterated in parallel at the same index** (existing two-slice entries — `clever_057_common`, `clever_046_median`, `clever_072_smallest_change` — all use sequential outer/inner composition or two-slice symmetry as a postcondition, never lockstep parallel iteration). The proof effectively lifts `abs_diff_i64_modified`'s scalar two-arm `if a≥b then a-b else b-a` shape into a slice-scan composition, with each arm carrying its own `BitVec.ssubOverflow` discharge — a generic template for any elementwise binary-operation-of-two-slices contract.
